narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 58% chance of not passing. Picture your journey like a story the model pieced together from key chapters of your background, starting with your academic standing: your Year 1 decile of 4.0 was the second-biggest signal pulling toward struggle, suggesting early hurdles in keeping up with the pack, but it was your Year 3 decile of 4.0 that weighed heaviest, whispering that even after time to adjust, you were still in that middle-of-the-road spot rather than climbing higher. Layered on top was your low family income, the third strongest thread, which the model associates with extra barriers like fewer resources for tutoring or stress-free study. Your LSAT score of 20.0 added to the caution, signaling a foundational challenge in the analytical skills bar prep demands, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 reinforced that pattern of solid but not standout performance. As an Asian female going full-time, these details together nudged the story further toward potential pitfalls, with your race and gender as mid-level influences and full-time status as a smaller one, perhaps hinting at the intense grind ahead without part-time flexibility. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they intertwined to paint a picture of resilience tested against steep odds. This isn't the end of your story; many with similar profiles have pivoted by targeting bar-specific prep or gap-year boosts. You've got target_law drive at 1.0, which shines through—consider retaking the LSAT or building that decile momentum elsewhere. We're rooting for your rewrite.
